title: Two yellow ribbons
summary: On a night train from Paris to Berlin, two strangers discover their bags are nearly identical. They make one small mark to tell them apart.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Excuse me. Is this seat yours?

body.speaker: Anna
body.text: Yes. My bag is on it. I will move it.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Don't move it. Sit. I have only this small bag.

body.speaker: Anna
body.text: Where do you go?

body.speaker: David
body.text: Berlin. You?

body.speaker: Anna
body.text: Berlin. The same train, the same city.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Your bag is exactly like mine.

body.speaker: Anna
body.text: Both green. Both old. Both have a yellow ribbon.

body.speaker: David
body.text: My wife tied this ribbon. So I can find my bag at the airport.

body.speaker: Anna
body.text: My father tied mine. For the same reason.

body.speaker: David
body.text: We have the same idea.

body.speaker: Anna
body.text: We have different families with the same idea.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Where are you from?

body.speaker: Anna
body.text: Stockholm. You?

body.speaker: David
body.text: Manchester. I work in Berlin. I go home every two weeks.

body.speaker: Anna
body.text: I work in Stockholm. My mother is in Berlin. She is eighty-four. I go every three weeks.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Do you sleep on the train?

body.speaker: Anna
body.text: A little. I read a little. I look out the window a lot. The window is the best part. It is dark. The towns pass. You see one window with a light on. Somebody is awake.

body.speaker: David
body.text: I have not thought about it like that.

body.speaker: Anna
body.text: I have travelled this train for nine years. The window is a thing I think about.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Where is your bag going? Berlin Hauptbahnhof?

body.speaker: Anna
body.text: Yes. Yours?

body.speaker: David
body.text: The same. We will be very confused.

body.speaker: Anna
body.text: My ribbon has a small bell.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Mine has no bell. Now I know mine.

body.speaker: Anna
body.text: Good. Now we sleep.

annotations.meaningVi: dải ruy băng (dây buộc trang trí)
Ga trung tâm (tiếng Đức, cho ga chính ở Berlin)
đã buộc / cột (quá khứ của tie)

annotations.phrase: I have not thought about it
annotations.meaningVi: Tôi chưa từng nghĩ đến điều đó (hiện tại hoàn thành phủ định)
tôi đã đi / đã đi lại (hiện tại hoàn thành — hành động kéo dài đến nay)
trôi qua, lướt qua (khi nhìn từ trên tàu)

grammarNotes.title: Possessive pronouns: my/your vs. mine/yours
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Có hai dạng sở hữu: 'my', 'your' đi với danh từ ('my bag', 'your seat'); 'mine', 'yours' đứng một mình, thay cho 'my + danh từ' đã nhắc trước. Trong bài: 'Is this seat yours?' = 'Is this your seat?' 'Your bag is exactly like mine.' = '… exactly like my bag.' 'My father tied mine.' = '… tied my ribbon.' 'Mine has no bell.' = 'My ribbon has no bell.' Dùng dạng độc lập (mine/yours) để tránh lặp danh từ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Two possessive forms: 'my', 'your' go before a noun ('my bag', 'your seat'); 'mine', 'yours' stand alone and replace 'my/your + noun' already mentioned. The dialogue: 'Is this seat yours?' = 'Is this your seat?' 'Your bag is exactly like mine.' = '… like my bag.' 'My father tied mine.' = '… tied my ribbon.' 'Mine has no bell.' = 'My ribbon has no bell.' Use mine/yours to avoid repeating the noun.

translationVi:
David: Xin lỗi. Ghế này là của cô không?
Anna: Vâng. Túi tôi trên đó. Tôi sẽ chuyển nó.
David: Đừng chuyển. Cô ngồi đi. Tôi chỉ có cái túi nhỏ này.
Anna: Anh đi đâu?
David: Berlin. Còn cô?
Anna: Berlin. Cùng chuyến tàu, cùng thành phố.
David: Túi cô giống y hệt túi tôi.
Anna: Cả hai cùng xanh. Cùng cũ. Cùng có một dải ruy băng vàng.
David: Vợ tôi buộc dải này. Để tôi tìm được túi mình ở sân bay.
Anna: Cha tôi buộc dải của tôi. Cùng lý do.
David: Hai gia đình có cùng ý tưởng.
Anna: Hai gia đình khác nhau với cùng một ý tưởng.
David: Cô từ đâu đến?
Anna: Stockholm. Còn anh?
David: Manchester. Tôi làm việc ở Berlin. Hai tuần một lần tôi về nhà.
Anna: Tôi làm ở Stockholm. Mẹ tôi ở Berlin. Bà tám mươi tư tuổi. Ba tuần một lần tôi đến thăm.
David: Cô có ngủ trên tàu không?
Anna: Một chút. Tôi đọc một chút. Tôi nhìn ra cửa sổ nhiều. Cửa sổ là phần hay nhất. Trời tối. Các thị trấn trôi qua. Bạn thấy một ô cửa sáng đèn. Có ai đó đang thức.
David: Tôi chưa từng nghĩ vậy.
Anna: Tôi đi chuyến tàu này chín năm rồi. Cửa sổ là điều tôi suy nghĩ về.
David: Túi cô về đâu? Berlin Hauptbahnhof?
Anna: Vâng. Còn anh?
David: Cũng vậy. Hai mình sẽ rất loạn.
Anna: Dải băng của tôi có một cái chuông nhỏ.
David: Của tôi không có chuông. Giờ tôi biết của tôi rồi.
Anna: Tốt. Giờ mình ngủ.

questions.prompt: Why are the bags the same?
options: Both are green, old, and have a yellow ribbon — tied by family members for the same reason
Both belong to the same person
They came from the same shop
questions.explanation: 'Both green. Both old. Both have a yellow ribbon.'

questions.prompt: Where is David from? Where does he work?
options: From Manchester; works in Berlin; goes home every two weeks
questions.explanation: 'Manchester. I work in Berlin. I go home every two weeks.'
questions.hint: David's reply about where he is from.

questions.prompt: Why does Anna go to Berlin every three weeks?
options: Her mother is in Berlin and is eighty-four.
She has a part-time job there.
questions.explanation: 'My mother is in Berlin. She is eighty-four. I go every three weeks.'

questions.prompt: What does Anna like best about the train?
options: The window — towns pass, sometimes one lit window means somebody is awake
questions.explanation: 'The window is the best part… one window with a light on. Somebody is awake.'
questions.hint: Anna's longer turn about the window.

questions.prompt: How long has Anna taken this train?
questions.explanation: 'I have travelled this train for nine years.'

questions.prompt: How will they tell the bags apart at Hauptbahnhof?
options: Anna's ribbon has a small bell; David's does not.
David will carry his bag in his hand.
They will mark them with a pen.
questions.explanation: 'My ribbon has a small bell.' / 'Mine has no bell.'

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: Strangers who share a long journey can find, in five minutes, that two different lives have been doing very similar small things for the same human reasons.
questions.explanation: Two unconnected families chose the same small habit; the discovery makes them strangers-with-something-in-common.
questions.hint: Think about the 'different families with the same idea' line.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Anna and David discover that two strangers from two countries have made the same small choice for the same reason — to be findable by family. Is there a small thing on an object you own that someone in your family put there for you? What would they say if you told them another stranger had the same thing?
